findings.description: parse() builds a column-name->position `index` dict but never uses it; rows are read positionally (row[0..3]) while validation only checks column existence as a set, so a CSV with reordered-but-present columns (e.g. header 'email,name,amount,id') passes validation and silently maps fields to the wrong attributes or raises ValueError on int(email).

count_rows returns len(lines) - 1, so an empty file yields -1 (and a header-only file yields 0 indistinguishably); the unguarded subtraction produces a negative/incorrect row count for empty or no-header inputs.
count_rows counts physical newlines via readlines() instead of CSV records, so any quoted field containing an embedded newline inflates the count and disagrees with parse()'s real CSV row semantics.

int(row[0]) / float(row[2]) have no per-row error handling, so a single non-numeric or empty cell (e.g. '', 'N/A', '$5') raises an unhandled ValueError that aborts the entire parse of an otherwise-valid file.
Rows are indexed up to row[3] with no length check, so a ragged/short row (fewer than 4 fields) or an empty trailing line raises an unhandled IndexError and crashes the whole parse.
open(path).readlines() never closes the file handle (no context manager), leaking a descriptor on each call.

export uses os.path.basename(filename); an empty filename or one ending in '/' yields basename '' and open() then targets the directory itself, raising IsADirectoryError rather than producing a usable file.
count_rows opens a caller-supplied path with no validation (path-traversal/arbitrary-read surface); flagged in passing only — defer detailed assessment to critic-security.
